item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When powered down, SCP-1939 becomes inoperative, and it ceases to receive any transmissions.

That's not how radios work. An unpowered radio should still have a tiny wire vibrating to received transmissions. It's the amp that stops.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Started off pretty good, but the interview was genuinely chilling - +1 for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral tethering on a downvote. I can like alt history and don't even fundamentally dislike "turns you into a Nazi (puppet) (maybe)", but it's played painfully straight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, it's because I'm an alt history buff that I hate this. (I managed to study it for one course in university, even — go me.) It's the same basic alt history setup seen a million times, but without any interesting detail, and with a rather 'meh' mind-control effect.

I tried to downvote, but found I already downvoted years ago. Ah well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love this and really wish it was higher rated.

Guess it's the history/alternate history buff in me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's The Man in the High Castle but without any interesting twists, it's too long and uninteresting, the tone is odd in some places and the grammar in others. Downvoted.

Also, and correct me if I'm wrong, but aren't statistically insignificant effects and no effects the same thing in this context?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: e: Thought better of this as soon as I posted it. Never mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edison is a charlatan and a plagiarist
Tesla could have saved the world

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my headcanon, the radio doesn't just 'turn you into' a Nazi. The only anomalous property it has is from being in another timeline. The reason the broadcasts make people so susceptible to their influence isn't because of some mystical anomalous property we can't understand, it's because in a world where the National Socialists won the war, they were able to fine-tune their psychological research to the point where they were able to manipulate the listener beyond the level of the Foundation universes ability to understand.

I like this article, however, I'd like to see some radio transcripts. Simply put it's something the big F would include in a scientific document about the thing.

No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I do agree with some others that this does hold itself as two SCP's, I think it holds up well and +1.

However : "nor does placing it deep underground, where radio waves should be unable to penetrate." This should give experimental perimeters, how deep should be effective and how deep was tried.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't mind that it's Nazis, but I feel like you have two separate ideas here that don't really gel together into a compelling whole. You have a radio from a parallel earth picking up alternate history broadcasts, and you've got a radio that turns people into Nazis. Personally, I think the first concept is the stronger of the two. No vote either way for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting idea, but I'd like to see some transcripts of what's being broadcasted, rather than just naming the shows off (if the researchers have already transcribed the radio shows, can we please have at least one example?). It might break up the text a bit with scripts of alternate history news reports.

I'm going to wait until later to vote on this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably "Fatherland", judging by the news broadcasts…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not just a radio that receives broadcasts from the Naziverse; you need to read the subtext: The radio was made around the time the Nazis gained prominence in Germany, listening to it turns you into a Nazi or someone with similar beliefs who follows orders without question, and when it wore off the D-Class gave the exact same excuse that was used during the Nuremberg trials.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this but I simply can't. I love the idea of alternative timelines, Axis forces winning WW2 and really don't mind them becoming a cliche around here. Unfortunately it seems like you focus more on the boring effects of the broadcasts than the broadcasts themselves.

I'm downvoting it for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So basically it's a radio that plays broadcasts from an alternate timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this one is successful, I may leave it 'til the 2001-2999 block opens up (or at least a good few months) because it's really too similar. But otherwise, I'll have to write it up.

I really do need to write an SCP at some point, haven't done one yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You, sir, have had an excellent idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
thats all well and good, but Tesla was the original person to theorize the usefullness of alternating current, when he pitched the idea to the world's other leading electrician (edison) he was laughed out of the building.

Sucks that he wound up dead in a ditch, I think he was the most under appreciated genius in history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have just had the best thought.

Could this be the "naziverse" that they were looking for in SCP-2559-J?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actually thinking of, for the British Empire, having the new imperial anthem (written in 19██ by Sir █████ Marshall) be the centre of the effect, with lesser effects scattered throughout the rest of the broadcast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Here's one you can copy-paste: ü

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The umlaut? I can't find the character on Office 2010.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly! Why doesn't anyone ever put the dots over the u like they should?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: B52Ltd the tone is alright. As for alternative things, I'd consider not having death involved, since "makes you crazy and want to kill people" is almost as boring as "makes you a Nazi."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's brilliant, either with a British Empire(so their atl-verse looks steampunk?) or the Nazis. How to work in though? Perhaps an addendum or note to the effect of "effect appears similar to prototype weapon & schematics designed by Dr. Suchundsuch recovered by Allied troops while securing a German reseach facility near Innsbruck in 1945. Records indicated died of pnuemonia in April, 1943." Couple with a mention in a radiobroadcast of a State Funeral being held for the man with the same name at some point after World War Two in their universe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not a massive fan, but your comment gave me an idea that makes me like it more.

What if they're not two unconnected thing. What if the Nazi's won WW2 in that world because they developed weaponised memetics?

Not sure if that's intended, as it's not very firmly hinted.

If they're two seperate things, it's just odd. But the idea that the war was changed by weaponised memetics is interesting.
Hmm, if this one gets downvoted I might try and write that up with the British Empire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm okay with the "What if the Nazis won" alternate history, but not the mind-affecting thing. That seems thoroughly tacked-on and unnecessary. What this really needs is more detailed description of the broadcasts. Or, y'know, something slightly more original, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So the general consensus is, "should have picked something other than Nazis"… fair enough. Two questions, 1) did I at least get the tone right? 2) As I am considering a major edit to a more colorful alternative universe, (I really liked the aztec idea), how does the psychological effect part of the SCP strike people, I think it need something like that, otherewise why bother to contain it? I mean, so we can hear what the Aztecs are up to these days, so what if they can't get here? I am thinking of keeping it have induce a religious fervor which induces a desire to engage in human sacrifice?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, I'd like to see this SCP, working with a less-obvious alternative history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoting, because an alternate universe radio is just made less interesting by the addition of Nazis…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To hell with that! I'd *love* to read an SCP indicating the great Aztec/Roman War of Australian Settlement, circa 1887. Or the way that Frederic Liebowitz was the first elected Jewish president of the Confederated States of America. Or how the influential German herbalist, Dr. Adolph Hitler, who (despite an initial interest in artistry) discovered that his passion for a vegetarian lifestyle had other health benefits, and moved from an impoverished Austria to set up what would become a significant system of health clinics throughout Eastern Europe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, Pasteur invented pasteurization, duh on me.

Not getting the point across to the reader would be a valid concern in antoher format. But for an scp you can just have a line that spells it out. "SCP-XXXX receives signals from a divergent time-line where the Aztec empire repelled the Conquistadors and formed the basis of a theocracy that currently occupies all of North America." That sort of on-the-nose fits just fine for a scientific summary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the reason people pick a German victory in World War Two(certainly the reason I did) is that if one wants to have an alternate universe in whatever media, it needs to be something instantly recognizable and with broad cultural currency, something capable of being grasped even by those with only a limited knowledge of history. How would the world be different if Alexander Fleming(not Pastuer) hadn't discovered penicilin? More infections to be sure, perhaps even world historicals figures would have been dead from them, but people would only recognize it as a different universe because of the political/military/economic effect, and since relatively few events have a the cultural currency World War Two does, it would likely manifest as the change that people would recognize most. Sure a Roman survived the Dark Ages would have vastly altered world history, but when you started talking about Coptic Christianity being the dominant religion of North Africa, or a Catholic(as a opposed to Orthodox) Russia or unifed Aztec Mexico fighting off Roman Legions, I think you'd lose most people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me want to write something where a time traveler goes back to WWII to meet Hitler, and instead of assassinating him, tells him that in the future, Nazis have been invoked so many times in literature, TV, movies, politics, discussions, etc. that they've been rendered boring and meaningless. Hopefully we could get a Darth Vader-style "NOOOOOOOOO" out of him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh dear. I like this, but I'm tempted to cancel my vote over this. That, and the whole alternate spelling of "Fuhrer".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow it's nice that I am not the only person who gets asked that :3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My thoughts exactly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, Furher.
Alternate universe has alternate german.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god how did I miss that? Downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Missing object class?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's not my argument. I was having a problem with the person calling it a 'control group' when it is clearly not a control group. Please to be reading what my posts actually say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As soon as I saw the word "German" I knew where this was headed. As others have pointed out, "alternate history where Germany won WWII," is a big cliche. The rest of the article would have to be pretty spectacular to get a pass, but unfortunately it isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the Foundation, turning something off doesn't always make it stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No power lines, but every building has a constelation of receivers for the wireless DC electricity.
Air travel is impossible because of all the power being broadcast.
Brain cancer is rampant as everyone is saturated with low-level radiation constantly (think cell-phone tumors).
No nuclear weapons, but all the major world powers have directed energy weapons, destructive power rated in "kilo-tunguskas".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See! See!
This is what I'm saying, there is plenty of interesting variations on history, with lots of room for different interpretations in each one. Any of them would be better than Nazi-world, because that particular concept ahs been done to death.

Side-note: I would love this if it was a radio that picked up the student radio stations from Alexylva U.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When powered down, SCP-1939 becomes inoperative, and it ceases to receive any transmissions.

The "and it ceases…" clause is unnecessary. In fact, do you really need this sentence at all? After all, most normal radios stop receiving if you switch them off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: True. In my mind, Greece did to Rome in Alexylva's universe what Rome did to Greece in ours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suspect it's both. For one, they still use latin script.
And to be honest, romans did absorb a lot of greek civillisation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tesla would be an interesting one to see. Tesla coils everywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought Alexylva was the Greeks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if the Roman empire hadn't fallen?

That's the point of Alexylva University, duh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why is every alternate history the one where the Germans won WW2?
It's a fairly interesting turning point, but there are soooo many other equally interesting possibilities that haven't been run into the ground.

What if Pasteur had just tossed that contaminated petri dish? We not only wouldn't have penicilin, we may not have antibiotics at all.
What if Tesla got famous and popular instead of Edison?
What if the Roman empire hadn't fallen? Or didn't rise in the first place? Or was based in the Caribean instead of the Mediteranean?
What if Japan was the dominant power after WW2?

My point is, this is executed well enough, but I've heard this song before. Change the station.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is gold. I guess when the day comes when quality anomalous media is downvoted, we will have the end of the world. Oh hey, it's 2012 already.

Now, what I like about this? Just like the EK-class radio and others, this is anohter piece you have no idea why it does what it does, but what it does is interesting. Because one can relate to it and see the mysterious parts for himself. It would be cool enough even without the psychological effect, that is a bit over the top for me, but I can't say that would deny me upvoting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree that it's not a control, but it's probably the right test. They know the broadcasts induce anomalous effects, they're trying to see whether it's the broadcast content or if hearing the broadcast from the anomalous radio itself is necessary. You could do that control as well (maybe playing some people German-language news bulletins from this world) as well to introduce another control, but for this specific experiment it's not really necessary.

Suggestion: the other obvious test to do would be to have a group of people standing close to SCP-1939 switched on in a soundproofed case to see whether it's proximity to the SCP's presence that causes the effect or whether you actually have to hear the broadcast.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't care for it personally.

Also:

recordings of these broadcasts played to a control group

This is not how control groups actually work. Control groups would probably listen to an unrelated radio (like hip hop or some boring news shit) or listen to complete silence. This acutally really bothers me, because we learned this shit in like, highschool science.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does it need any more than that?

Think of how if would affect the world uncontained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. A radio that turns you (albeit, temporarily) into a National Socialist. It's an idea I can dig, but this article is just kinda there.

I'm not going to vote because anomalous radio broadcasts are creepy to me in and of themselves, so I like it a bit based on that alone. But I think this needs something more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. When I searched the site for radios, I found three, one made the user deaf to all other sounds, one picked up radio broadcasts from the past, and the last from another universe. The last two were interesting, but didn't really seem dangerous. Thought I might my take would be to make it dangerous, something in need of containtment. But if it doesn't seem that different to you, I get it.
